items.question: If I had studied medicine, I ___ a doctor now.
explanations: 'Would have been' keeps the result in the past, but 'now' is present.
Correct! Past condition, present result: 'I'd be a doctor now'.
'Will be' is a real future, not this imagined present.
'Am' is present simple, but this is a hypothetical result.

items.question: If she ___ the offer, she'd be living abroad today.
explanations: 'Accepted' (past simple) is the second conditional; a past condition needs the past perfect.
Correct! Past condition uses the past perfect: 'If she had accepted the offer'.
'Accepts' is present simple, but the condition is in the past.
The if-clause never uses 'would'.

items.question: If I weren't so shy, I ___ her out last week.
explanations: 'Would ask' points to now, but the result ('last week') is in the past.
Correct! Present condition, past result: 'I would have asked her out last week'.
'Will ask' is a real future, not this imagined past.
'Asked' (past simple) doesn't express the imagined result.

items.question: If he ___ afraid of flying, he would have visited us last summer.
explanations: Correct! A permanent present condition takes the past simple: 'If he weren't afraid of flying'.
'Hadn't been' would make it a normal third conditional about the past, not his ongoing fear.
'Isn't' is present simple, but this is hypothetical.
The if-clause never uses 'would'.

items.question: If I hadn't missed the train, I ___ at work already.
explanations: 'Would have been' keeps it in the past, but 'already/now' is present.
Correct! Past condition, present result: 'I'd be at work already'.
'Am' is present simple, but this is a hypothetical result.
'Will be' is a real future, not this imagined present.

items.question: If we ___ more careful with money, we'd own a house by now.
explanations: 'Were' (past simple) is the second conditional; a past condition needs the past perfect.
Correct! Past condition uses the past perfect: 'If we had been more careful'.
'Are' is present simple, but the condition is in the past.
The if-clause never uses 'would'.

items.question: Correct this: 'If I had learnt to drive, I would have a car now.'
options: If I had learnt to drive, I would have had a car now.
If I learnt to drive, I would have a car now.
If I have learnt to drive, I would have a car now.
explanations: Correct! Past condition + present result: 'If I had learnt to drive, I would have a car now'.
'Would have had' wrongly puts the result in the past; 'now' is present.
'If I learnt' is the second conditional, not a past condition.
'If I have learnt' is present perfect, which doesn't fit here.

items.question: If I ___ so tired, I would have enjoyed the party.
explanations: 'Hadn't been' makes it a standard third conditional about that night; 'weren't' shows an ongoing state.
Correct! A general present state + past result: 'If I weren't so tired (in general), I would have enjoyed the party'.
'Am not' is present simple, but this is hypothetical.
The if-clause never uses 'would'.

items.question: Rewrite: 'I didn't sleep well, so I can't concentrate now.'
options: If I had slept well, I could concentrate now.
If I slept well, I could concentrate now.
If I had slept well, I could have concentrated now.
If I sleep well, I can concentrate now.
explanations: Correct! Past condition, present result: 'If I had slept well, I could concentrate now'.
'If I slept well' is the second conditional, not a past condition.
'Could have concentrated now' wrongly puts the result in the past.
'If I sleep well, I can concentrate' is a zero conditional (a general truth).

items.question: If they had booked earlier, they ___ on the beach right now.
explanations: 'Would have relaxed' keeps it in the past, but 'right now' is present.
Correct! Past condition, present result: 'they'd be relaxing on the beach right now'.
'Relax' is present simple, but this is a hypothetical result.
'Will relax' is a real future, not this imagined present.
